title: Perfect competition foundational concepts
description: Practice what you have learned about the foundational ideas behind perfectly competitive markets, including the characteristics of a perfectly competitive market, profit-maximization, and graphs of perfectly competitive firms.
topic_path: Production, cost, and the perfect competition model

assessment_items.question_content:
**All of the following are characteristics of perfectly competitive markets EXCEPT**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Which of the following is a characteristic of perfectly competitive markets?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: firms set the price they charge

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best defines **market power**?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the ability to control the price of a good
a firm’s ability to control the quantity they produce
the ability to make a profit in the short run
a firm’s decision to enter or exit a market

assessment_items.question_content:
**How is the price in a perfectly competitive market determined?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: by the strategic interaction of firms

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes a firm’s demand curve in a perfectly competitive market?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

This graph represents the market for pencils, which is perfectly competitive:

![A graph illustrates the relationship between price (P) and quantity (Q) in millions. The vertical axis represents price, labeled as "P," while the horizontal axis represents quantity, labeled "Q (in millions)." Two lines are depicted on the graph. The downward-sloping line is labeled "D," which represents the demand curve. A horizontal line marked at the price level of 1 dollar intersects the demand curve. The upward-sloping line is labeled "S," representing the supply curve. The intersection point of the demand and supply curves is marked by a dot. A dotted vertical line extends from the equilibrium point down to the quantity axis, intersecting it at 50 million.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0c416d0b283ba42de018a07379bb5c75a09a1c2a)

**Which of the following best represents the demand, marginal revenue, and costs curves for a *single* firm in this market?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes the profit-maximizing rule for a perfectly competitive firm?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Choose the quantity where marginal cost equals price.
Choose the quantity where marginal cost equals average total cost.
Choose the price where its marginal revenue is highest.
Choose the quantity where marginal revenue equals average total cost.
Choose the price where average total cost equals average revenue.

assessment_items.question_content:
This graph illustrates cost and revenue curves for a firm in a perfectly competitive market.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the quantity that this firm will produce, and what price will it charge?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A graph plots cost curves in a market structure, with Quantity on the horizontal axis ranging from 0 to 1000 units and Cost in dollars on the vertical axis ranging from 0 dollars to 110 dollars. It includes three curves: Marginal Cost (M C), Average Total Cost (A T C), and Average Variable Cost (A V C), along with a horizontal Demand equals Marginal Revenue (D equals M R) line at 110 dollars. The A V C curve starts at 32 dollars for a quantity of 0, gradually increases, and approaches the A T C curve. The A T C curve starts above the A V C curve, reaching 50 dollars at a quantity of 600 units. The M C curve begins below the A T C and A V C curves, intersects the A T C curve at approximately 600 units and the D equals M R line at a quantity of 1000 units and a cost of 110 dollars. Key intersections are marked at 32 dollars for 400 units on the A V C curve, 60 dollars for 600 units on the A T C curve, and 110 dollars for 1000 units on the M C curve.

assessment_items.question_content:
Atmos enterprises produces watches in a perfectly competitive market. Its cost and revenue curves are shown in this graph.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4a3a5bf204b5cfbd774f3d837522ff645d0df386)

**What quantity will this firm produce, and what price will it charge, in the short run?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**If a firm in a perfectly competitive market is earning normal economic profits, which of the following is true?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

This graph shows the cost curves and marginal revenue curves for a firm.

[[☃ image 1]]

**How much is this firm’s profit?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
To find the profit:

1. Find the profit maximizing quantity
2. Find the average total cost of that quantity. 
3. Calculate profit:

$\text{Profit}=Q \times (P-ATC)$
The profit-maximizing quantity is the quantity where marginal cost and marginal revenue intersect.
The profit-maximizing quantity is $10{,}000$:

[[☃ image 1]]


The average total cost of $10{,}000$ is $\$80$:

[[☃ image 1]]

Profit is the rectangle that represents the quantity sold multiplied by the difference between price and average total cost:

$\text{Profit}=\text{Quantity} \times (\text{Price} - \text{Average Total Cost})$

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} \text{Profit}&= \text{Quantity} \times (\text{Price} - \text{Average total cost})\\\\
&=10,000 \times (\$120 - \$80)\\\\
& =10,000 \times \$40\\\\
&=\$400{,}000\end{align}$

options.alt: A line graph depicts the relationship between cost and quantity for a firm. The graph has a vertical axis labeled "Cost" and a horizontal axis labeled "Quantity in thousands." There are four labeled curves and a horizontal line at 120 dollars on the vertical axis. The horizontal line is labeled "D equals M R." The curve labeled "M C" starts low, below the D-line, slopes upward, and increases steeply as it moves to the right. The curve labeled "A T C" has a U-shape, starting above the D-line, decreasing to a low point, and then increasing to the top right of the graph. The curve labeled "A V C" starts below the A T C curve and increases to the top right of the graph. The graph includes several key intersections and markings. Curves A V C and A T C intersect at (3, 25). Curves M C and A T C intersect at (8, 75). Line D and curve M C intersect at (10, 120). Dashed lines extend downward from the intersection points to the horizontal axis, marking quantities 3, 8, and 10 in thousands. Horizontal dashed lines extend from the intersection points to the vertical axis, marking cost levels 25, 75, and 80 dollars.

assessment_items.question_content:
This graph shows the cost curves and marginal revenue curves for a firm.

[[☃ image 1]]

**How much is this firm’s short-run profit?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The find the profit:
1. Find the profit maximizing quantity
2. Find the average total cost of that quantity. 
3. Calculate profit:

$\text{Profit}=Q \times (P-ATC)$
The profit-maximizing quantity is the quantity where marginal cost and marginal revenue intersect.

The average total cost of $4{,}000$ is $\$101$:

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6efccba13b910a162e901bfdacebd4555ab0cf45)

Profit is the rectangle that represents the quantity sold multiplied by the difference between price and average total cost:

$\text{Profit}=\text{Quantity} \times (\text{Price} - \text{Average Total Cost}$

![A line graph depicts the relationship between cost and quantity for a firm. The graph has a vertical axis labeled "Cost" and a horizontal axis labeled "Quantity in thousands." There are four labeled curves and a horizontal line at 30 dollars on the vertical axis. The horizontal line is labeled "D equals M R." The curve labeled "M C" starts low, below the D-line, slopes upward, and increases steeply as it moves to the right. The curve labeled "A T C" has a U-shape, starting above the D-line, decreasing to a low point, and then increasing to the top right of the graph. The curve labeled "A V C" starts below the A T C curve and increases to the top right of the graph. The graph includes several key intersections and markings. Intersection points are highlighted with black dots where the curves intersect with the D-line and with each other. The first intersection point is at quantity 3 in thousands and cost 25 dollars. The second intersection point is at quantity 4 in thousands and cost 75 dollars, where the A T C curve touches the D-line. The third intersection point is at quantity 8 in thousands and cost 101 dollars, where the M C curve intersects the A T C curve. Dashed lines extend downward from the intersection points to the horizontal axis, marking quantities 3, 4, and 8 in thousands. Horizontal dashed lines extend from the intersection points to the vertical axis, marking cost levels 25, 75, and 101 dollars. An arrow points to a shaded triangle between cost 101 and the D line. The arrow represents Profit.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e6eccc933f435d0b487e5802cbcd5ddd0fb90273)
